feedback.text: Dear European Commission. I make this appeal to you because video games are very dear to my heart. They are often more than just mindless pastimes; they allow stories to be told in an interactive way that isn't possible with other media forms such as books or films. But while books, films, and many other media forms are carefully archived, and their cultural influence is preserved for posterity in archives, this is often not possible with video games. In a form of planned or carelessly accepted obsolescence, many modern video games are created in such a way that they rely on the operation of infrastructure (e.g., servers) by the game manufacturer. If the manufacturer decides that the continued operation of this infrastructure is no longer profitable and ceases operations, the consumers who purchased the video game lose access to it. In most cases, without financial compensation. The cultural value that the game potentially had is thus also lost, since mere archiving without the necessary infrastructure does not allow for playback. This practice, based on the premise that customers only purchase a license, which can be invalidated by the manufacturer at any time, creates a blatant power imbalance between consumers and game manufacturers. Therefore, I appeal to you to advocate for game manufacturers to be required to provide a way to make video games available to consumers in a form that retains their core functionality, even after the manufacturer ceases operations.
